Class ProcessDefinitionService

java.lang.Object
de.hallerweb.enterprise.prioritize.service.process.ProcessDefinitionService
@Service public class ProcessDefinitionService extends Object
Registry of the BPMN process definitions the platform knows about. Registering links an existing document — the BPMN source with its version history — to a process key; it does not deploy anything. Deployment is a separate, deliberate act (see the activation slice).

Authorization follows the createProject precedent: a process definition has no owning container to authorize against, so it is gated by a type-level permission — a PermissionRecord with objectId == 0 on ProcessDefinition. Admins are allowed implicitly and no default grant is seeded, so a fresh installation starts closed. Reading the underlying document is authorized by the documents subsystem itself, as it always was.

Author:
peter haller

  • Constructor Details

    • ProcessDefinitionService

      public ProcessDefinitionService(ProcessDefinitionRepository definitionRepository, DocumentService documentService, BpmnDefinitionReader definitionReader, AuthorizationService authService, org.flowable.engine.RepositoryService repositoryService)

  • Method Details

    • register

      @Transactional public ProcessDefinitionDTO register(Long documentInfoId, PUser user)
      Registers the document's current version as a process definition, in state ProcessDefinitionState.DRAFT.

      The process key is read out of the file rather than taken from the caller — the diagram is the truth about what it defines. A key already in use is refused: which definition answers to a key must not depend on deployment order, and a collision is a mistake worth seeing immediately.

      Parameters:
      documentInfoId - the document carrying the BPMN source
      user - the authenticated caller
      Returns:
      the registered definition
      Throws:
      org.springframework.security.access.AccessDeniedException - if the caller may not register definitions or not read the document
      NoSuchElementException - if no such document exists
      IllegalArgumentException - if the document holds no usable BPMN process
      IllegalStateException - if the key is taken or the document is already registered

    • getAll

      @Transactional(readOnly=true) public List<ProcessDefinitionDTO> getAll(PUser user)
      All registered definitions, deployed or not.

    • get

      @Transactional(readOnly=true) public ProcessDefinitionDTO get(Long id, PUser user)
      A single definition.
      Throws:
      NoSuchElementException - if there is none with that id

    • activate

      @Transactional public ProcessDefinitionDTO activate(Long id, PUser user)
      Deploys the definition's current document version to the engine and makes it startable.

      This is the deliberate act the whole design turns on: revising a diagram does not deploy it, somebody with the permission has to say so. Activation is idempotent — activating a definition that is already active on the current document version changes nothing.

      A new deployment is created only when there is something new to deploy (never deployed before, or the document has a newer version since the last activation). Otherwise a suspended definition is simply resumed, so taking something out of service and back in does not pile up engine versions. The BPMN is re-read first: if the document's process key changed since registration, the file no longer defines the process this entry stands for and activation is refused.

      Throws:
      org.springframework.security.access.AccessDeniedException - if the caller may not activate definitions
      NoSuchElementException - if there is no such definition
      IllegalStateException - if the document's process key no longer matches

    • deactivate

      @Transactional public ProcessDefinitionDTO deactivate(Long id, PUser user)
      Takes an active definition out of service: no new instances can be started from it, while instances that are already running continue untouched. The deployment is never deleted — running instances are real work, and the engine keeps the definition they were started with.
      Throws:
      IllegalStateException - if the definition is not currently active

    • unregister

      @Transactional public void unregister(Long id, PUser user)
      Removes a definition from the registry.

      Only a ProcessDefinitionState.DRAFT definition can be unregistered. Once it has been deployed, the engine holds a deployment and possibly running instances, and this entity is the only thing mapping them back to their document — dropping it would leave that deployment unattributable. Deactivating a definition is the way to take it out of service.

      Throws:
      IllegalStateException - if the definition has been deployed
